meta.title: Rethinking Human-AI Collaboration in Complex Medical Decision Making: A Case Study in Sepsis Diagnosis
meta.authors: Zhang, Yu, Xu, Yin, Lu, Yao, Tory, Padilla, Caterino, Zhang & Wang

domains.name: Clinical medicine
domains.blurb: Sepsis, blood culture, vitals & labs, EHR, the ER/ICU workflow.

domains.name: Machine learning & AI
domains.blurb: Neural networks, LSTM, attention, prediction, explainability (XAI).

domains.name: HCI research methods
domains.blurb: Interview studies, qualitative coding, design probes, human-AI teaming.

domains.name: Statistics & evaluation
domains.blurb: Sensitivity, false positives, AUC, confidence intervals, entropy.

sections.heading: The smartest doctor in the building keeps getting ignored
The hardest call on the ward
What we already knew about AI in the clinic
Five reasons nobody trusts the alarm
Get out of the judge's seat

skeleton.text: Walk into almost any large hospital today and there is an artificial intelligence quietly watching every patient, trained to catch one of medicine's deadliest emergencies before a human can.
focus: Today's AI systems for medical decision support often succeed on benchmark datasets in research papers

skeleton.text: On paper it is brilliant. In practice, the doctors have learned to tune it out.
focus: but fail in real-world deployment.

skeleton.text: This study is about why that happens — and what to do instead.
focus: The study begins with a formative study investigating why clinical experts abandon an existing AI-powered Sepsis predictive module

skeleton.text: The researchers zoom in on sepsis, the body's catastrophic overreaction to an infection, which can turn a stable patient into a dying one within hours.
focus: yet life-threatening organ dysfunction triggered by a dysregulated response to infection

skeleton.text: The warning system most hospitals already own was built to flag it; the six clinicians here had essentially stopped believing it.
focus: sepsis prediction and alerting module, Epic Sepsis Module (ESM)
All physicians complain that the current sepsis prediction is too late and thus useless

skeleton.text: The authors' diagnosis is blunt: the AI was pointed at the wrong moment.
focus: Such output at the final decision stage implicitly "challenges" human decision-makers' authority and expertise in their roles of making that final decision.

skeleton.text: It tried to hand down the final verdict, when what doctors needed was a partner for the uncertain middle of the decision.
focus: Due to the current prediction focusing too much on the final stage of decision making only

skeleton.text: So the team rebuilt it — a tool called SepsisLab, designed to assist rather than judge — and put it back in front of those same clinicians.
focus: Therefore, we build SepsisLab based on a state-of-the-art AI algorithm and extend it to predict the future projection of sepsis development, visualize the prediction uncertainty, and propose actionable suggestions

skeleton.text: To see why this matters, you have to understand how merciless sepsis is.
focus: The development of sepsis is very fast — without a timely diagnosis or proper treatment, a patient might die within a few hours

skeleton.text: It strikes roughly 48.9 million people a year and kills around 11 million of them, which is why catching it early is everything.
focus: Sepsis is a common ( 48.9 million patients per year worldwide)

skeleton.text: Three forces make the call brutal at once: the stakes are life-or-death, the clock is sprinting, and the doctor must commit before the evidence has arrived.
focus: sepsis diagnosis is particularly challenging because
make a time-sensitive decision at the first encounter with the patients, despite they face huge uncertainty

skeleton.text: Reading a scan is hard, but the answer is at least somewhere in the image; a sepsis call asks two questions at once — does this patient have sepsis right now, and are they about to?
focus: clinicians need to decide not only whether the patient has sepsis at that moment, but also how likely this patient may develop sepsis in the near future

skeleton.text: The one test that settles it, a blood culture, takes at least eight hours to come back — an eternity when a patient can crash in less.
focus: the golden standard test for sepsis is the "blood culture" test in the Sepsis-3 guidelines. However, it takes at least 8 hours to obtain the result

skeleton.text: So in the real world doctors don't wait; they work in four quick steps — form a hunch, gather more data, test it against fresh lab results, then make the call.
focus: Among the four stages of the medical decision-making workflow (1. Generating hypotheses, 2. Gathering data, 3. Testing hypotheses, 4. Making decisions)

skeleton.text: Before building anything, the team looked at what was already known about putting AI in the clinic.
focus: numerous studies show such AI-CDSS can effectively assist doctors

skeleton.text: The pattern was familiar: these tools can help, but doctors stay wary — they carry the legal responsibility, the models are opaque, and bolting AI onto the records can even add work.
focus: a significant hurdle is liability: because clinicians bear responsibility for medical decisions, they approach AI predictions with caution

skeleton.text: And for sepsis in particular, earlier work warned the fix isn't fancier explanations — it's explanations that match how a doctor already thinks, so trust can form.
focus: the most important challenge is not to use XAI methods to explain the model outcome, but to use methods consistent with the doctor's cognition

skeleton.text: Into that pressure cooker drops the incumbent AI, the Epic Sepsis Module.
focus: One notable effort is that a number of hospitals recently started to adopt a sepsis prediction and alerting module, Epic Sepsis Module (ESM)

skeleton.text: It reads the patient's chart, boils everything down to a single sepsis "risk score," and sounds an alarm the moment that score crosses a line.
focus: take in a patient's EHR information and other biomarker data at that moment as input and predict a sepsis risk score as output. If the predicted risk score is higher than a threshold

skeleton.text: To find out why clinicians abandon it, the team ran a formative study — open-ended interviews with six doctors and nurses who live with the tool every day.
focus: We recruited six clinicians who are domain experts whose daily work involves sepsis diagnosis and who have used the Epic Sepsis Module (ESM).

skeleton.text: The six were ICU, ER, and internal-medicine staff who live with sepsis, recruited by word of mouth; each sat for a roughly 35-minute remote interview recalling a recent case.
focus: all are active physicians or nurses in the Intensive Care Unit, Emergency Department, or Internal Medicine

skeleton.text: Their complaints fell into five buckets, and the first four are brutal.
focus: All physicians complain that the current sepsis prediction is too late and thus useless in their decision-making.

skeleton.text: It's too late: the alert routinely arrives after the doctor has already spotted the sepsis and started treatment.
focus: AI prediction must rely on data as input, but at first encounter, many of such data (e.g., vitals or lab results) do not exist or are not digitalized yet.

skeleton.text: It's too noisy: tuned so low it never misses a case, the alarm is wrong the overwhelming majority of the times it fires, burying ER staff under more than twenty interruptions an hour.
focus: an extremely low sensitivity threshold at 13% (i.e., high false positive rate at 87%)
more than 20 interruptions per hour for an ER physician

skeleton.text: It won't explain itself: a black box that could rate an obvious case lower than a subtle one, ignoring the very criteria doctors were trained on.
focus: Participants do not understand why an obvious sepsis case has a lower risk score than the score of a less obvious case.

skeleton.text: And it's useless even when right: a high number never said what to do next, so most people simply ignored it.
focus: they simply ignored the risk score during their diagnosis process because they were confused about the purpose of the risk score and did not know what to do as an actionable next step

skeleton.text: But the fifth complaint cut deepest: by trying to make the diagnosis itself, the AI had cast itself not as a teammate but as an intimidating competitor.
focus: this human-AI interaction paradigm is essentially perceived as a human-AI competition, which challenges the human experts' expertise and intimidates their authorities

skeleton.text: Pulled together, the five complaints point one way: doctors want help in the messy middle of the decision, not a verdict at the end.
focus: human experts need AI to focus more on supporting their intermediate decision-making process, rather than predicting a final outcome

skeleton.text: That reframing is the heart of the paper: the cure isn't a smarter verdict, it's a different job.
focus: The current AI-based sepsis prediction module mainly focuses on the prediction of the final decision outcomes, in our case, it is the sepsis risk score

skeleton.text: Move the AI out of the final-answer seat and into the intermediate steps — the hunch-forming and data-gathering — where doctors genuinely want a hand.
focus: Design Strategy 5: Shifting AI from Suggesting the Final Decision to Supporting Intermediate Stages.

skeleton.text: From that single shift came five design rules: forecast where the risk is heading, explain itself in plain reach, hand over concrete next actions, show how unsure it is, and above all support the middle of the process instead of seizing the end.
focus: Design Strategy 1: Performing Future Risk Score Prediction. Design Strategy 2: Providing Accessible Model Explanation. Design Strategy 3: Revealing Actionable Insights and Suggestions. Design Strategy 4: Displaying Uncertainty beyond the Risk Score.

skeleton.text: SepsisLab is what those rules look like built into the existing records screen, and it adds three things the old score never had.
focus: one core part of SepsisLab is a predictive algorithm that generates future prediction of sepsis risk scores

skeleton.text: First, instead of a single number it draws a trajectory — a line showing where the patient's risk is heading over the next four hours, the forecast continuing past "now" as a dashed line.
focus: we design a time-series plot to visualize both the historical (the solid line) and expected future (the dashed line) risk prediction trajectory over time

skeleton.text: Second, it never shows that number naked: a grey band widens around the line wherever the model is unsure, turning raw confidence into honest doubt.
focus: the model also generates the uncertainty range of the expected value, as shown in the gray area in the line plot

skeleton.text: Third — the part clinicians loved most — it tells you which lab tests to order next, ranked by how much each one would shrink that uncertainty.
focus: we added the lab tests recommendation function into our interface. SepsisLab recommends an item list ranked by their importance

skeleton.text: It will even play what-if: click a test you haven't run, and SepsisLab previews how the prediction would likely move — a counterfactual — before you spend the time and cost of doing it.
focus: observe how their test results could influence the model's risk prediction trajectory (the red dashed line) and the corresponding uncertainty range (the red area). Note that the red line and area are counterfactual values

skeleton.text: Beneath the interface, the engine is an LSTM, a neural network built to read a sequence over time — a natural fit for a patient's record, where readings dribble in at irregular hours across a stay.
focus: we select Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) as the backbone of the prediction framework, which is able to capture both long-term and short-term clinical information

skeleton.text: An attention mechanism lets it cope with the inevitable missing data, and doubles as the explanation: whatever it learns to pay attention to becomes the feature-importance list shown to the doctor.
focus: The attention module can automatically focus on important variables, and the learned attention weights can be used to interpret the prediction results

skeleton.text: To gauge its own uncertainty it quietly imagines the missing lab values many times over — a Monte Carlo simulation — and measures how much the answer jumps around.
focus: we adopt Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) to sample the missing values many times and compute the uncertainty with the standard deviation of the outputs

skeleton.text: When that wobble, captured as Shannon entropy, climbs too high, the system speaks up and recommends the single lab value that would settle it the most.
focus: We define the uncertainty as Shannon entropy e = -p log(p) - (1-p) log(1-p). If the uncertainty e > th_e, the model will recommend clinicians to collect more clinical variables

skeleton.text: That what-if isn't a guess: the model computes a counterfactual — re-running the forecast as if the test had been done — so the doctor can weigh a test's payoff before paying its time and cost.
focus: SepsisLab computes a counterfactual prediction: it estimates how the risk trajectory and its uncertainty would change if a recommended lab test were performed

skeleton.text: Tested against real ICU records, it nearly matched a model that could see everything, while asking for only about 9.6% more lab tests — and beat the data-starved baseline by roughly 10% in accuracy.
focus: our recommendation algorithm performs comparable to full observation setting models and outperforms the masked setting models by approximately 10% with only 9.6% extra laboratory values

skeleton.text: The real test was human: the same six clinicians took SepsisLab for a spin on de-identified cases, then talked it through.
focus: We recruited the same six clinical experts to perform a heuristic test with our system.

skeleton.text: They worked through ten de-identified MIMIC-III cases — five who turned out septic, five not — with the prototype standing in as a design probe.
focus: we selected two patient groups — one ultimately diagnosed with sepsis and one without — and randomly chose five data points from each, totaling ten cases

skeleton.text: The verdict was warm — pulling the AI out of the verdict seat made it feel less like a rival and more like a collaborator.
focus: the new system was able to improve the human-AI collaboration experience
ease feelings of being in competition with or replaced by AI

skeleton.text: They liked watching the risk unfold into the future ("it says the whole picture"), found the lab suggestions genuinely actionable for once, and said the what-if previews finally let them see the model's reasoning.
focus: All six participants liked the time-based prediction capability of our system.
All six participants responded positively to the lab test recommendation.
Participants agreed that showing counterfactual predictions based on the recommendation would drive their choice of lab tests.

skeleton.text: All six liked seeing risk as a trajectory: the graph "says the whole picture," showing not just now but where things head next.
focus: All six participants liked the time-based prediction capability of our system.

skeleton.text: All six found the lab-test suggestions genuinely actionable — they fit the existing workflow, and doctors said they'd actually order what the model asked for.
focus: All six participants responded positively to the lab test recommendation.
consistent with current physician workflows

skeleton.text: And the what-if previews built trust: seeing how a test would move the prediction helped them narrow the search instead of leaning on gut alone.
focus: being able to see the potential outcomes of different lab tests would help their thinking process and narrow the search space

skeleton.text: Their one real worry was dosage: don't drown us in information — hand it over a step at a time.
focus: Instead, our system ensures that physicians retain the role of final decision-maker.

skeleton.text: Step back and the lesson is about where AI sits: chasing the most accurate final score is exactly what broke collaboration; the win came from helping the steps before the verdict.
focus: we argue that AI should support human experts in the intermediate stages (Steps 1–3) rather than the final stage

skeleton.text: The takeaway reaches past one disease: wherever a decision is high-stakes, deeply uncertain, and racing a clock, AI seems to help most by supporting the process and least by grabbing the conclusion.
focus: there are a number of decision-making tasks that have similar properties as sepsis diagnosis: highly uncertain, high-stakes, and time-sensitive

skeleton.text: The same shape recurs in stroke and heart attacks, in mental-health crises, and even outside medicine in disaster response and business emergencies.
focus: Examples include both physical health problems (e.g., stroke, heart attack, and meningitis), and mental health problems
non-healthcare complex decision-making scenarios as well, such as military

skeleton.text: The authors don't oversell it: a tool like this can pile on cognitive load, can still be wrong in ways nobody has solved, and lean on it too hard and your own vigilance dulls.
focus: participants were concerned about the cognitive load caused by our system
mistakes and errors made by AI are inevitable

skeleton.text: And the evidence is early — one hospital, six clinicians, a prototype never wired into the live records system, with a parting hint that doctors and nurses, doing different jobs, may need different tools.
focus: We only involved six physicians in our formative study and heuristic evaluation, who came from the same hospital and only used one specific sepsis module ESM

skeleton.text: The bottom line: in fast, high-stakes, uncertain calls, AI helps most as a partner in the middle of the work — and SepsisLab is one concrete way to build that.
focus: AI should not focus on predicting the final decision, which can intimidate human experts

original.label: Abstract
original.text: Today's AI systems for medical decision support often succeed on benchmark datasets in research papers but fail in real-world deployment. This work focuses on the decision making of sepsis, an acute life-threatening systematic infection that requires an early diagnosis with high uncertainty from the clinician. Our aim is to explore the design requirements for AI systems that can support clinical experts in making better decisions for the early diagnosis of sepsis. The study begins with a formative study investigating why clinical experts abandon an existing AI-powered Sepsis predictive module in their electrical health record (EHR) system. We argue that a human-centered AI system needs to support human experts in the intermediate stages of a medical decision-making process (e.g., generating hypotheses or gathering data), instead of focusing only on the final decision. Therefore, we build SepsisLab based on a state-of-the-art AI algorithm and extend it to predict the future projection of sepsis development, visualize the prediction uncertainty, and propose actionable suggestions (i.e., which additional laboratory tests can be collected) to reduce such uncertainty. Through heuristic evaluation with six clinicians using our prototype system, we demonstrate that SepsisLab enables a promising human-AI collaboration paradigm for the future of AI-assisted sepsis diagnosis and other high-stakes medical decision making.

original.label: §2.1 Sepsis Diagnosis
original.text: Sepsis is a common ( 48.9 million patients per year worldwide) yet life-threatening organ dysfunction triggered by a dysregulated response to infection. The development of sepsis is very fast — without a timely diagnosis or proper treatment, a patient might die within a few hours from the initial onset of symptoms.

original.label: §1 Introduction
original.text: Compared to other medical decision-making scenarios (e.g., abnormal cell detection in medical imaging) where clinicians make a decision for that particular moment and with all the information they have at hand (e.g., cancer cells present or absent in the image), sepsis diagnosis is particularly challenging because: 1) clinicians need to decide not only whether the patient has sepsis at that moment, but also how likely this patient may develop sepsis in the near future (e.g., in a few hours); 2) they often do not have enough information to support their decision making. For example, the golden standard test for sepsis is the "blood culture" test in the Sepsis-3 guidelines. However, it takes at least 8 hours to obtain the result, which would most likely be too late for a patient with sepsis.

original.label: §1 Introduction (4-step workflow)
original.text: Among the four stages of the medical decision-making workflow (1. Generating hypotheses, 2. Gathering data, 3. Testing hypotheses, 4. Making decisions), this AI system is designed to provide support for the final diagnosis stage.

original.label: §1 Introduction (Epic Sepsis Module)
original.text: One notable effort is that a number of hospitals recently started to adopt a sepsis prediction and alerting module, Epic Sepsis Module (ESM), in their existing Electrical Health Record (EHR) system. The core of this sepsis module is a machine learning algorithm, which can take in a patient's EHR information and other biomarker data at that moment as input and predict a sepsis risk score as output. If the predicted risk score is higher than a threshold, it suggests to human clinicians that a sepsis case presents, and human clinicians can agree, disagree, or dismiss such AI decision-making suggestions.

original.label: §3.2.1 Belated Sepsis Risk Prediction
original.text: All physicians complain that the current sepsis prediction is too late and thus useless in their decision-making. This is due to the fact that sepsis is a life-threatening disease and can progress very fast. Thus, sepsis diagnosis requires human decision-makers to make a time-sensitive decision at the first encounter with the patients, despite they face huge uncertainty due to the lack of sufficient information about the patients. However, AI prediction must rely on data as input, but at first encounter, many of such data (e.g., vitals or lab results) do not exist or are not digitalized yet.

original.label: §3.2.2 Inaccurate Sepsis Risk Prediction
original.text: The current algorithm design tends to have an extremely low sensitivity threshold at 13% (i.e., high false positive rate at 87%) to avoid missing any potential sepsis, because sepsis decision making has a patient's life at stake. As a result, participants (P3, P4, P6) reported that they received an overly large volume of false alerts from the sepsis module due to the inaccurate sepsis risk prediction algorithm and the low sensitivity threshold. "Any system that produces tons of alerts will induce alert fatigue, and people won't pay attention to it ... on average, there are already more than 20 interruptions per hour for an ER physician." (P6)

original.label: §3.2.3 Lack of Explanations
original.text: Participants (P1, P4, P5, P6) also mentioned that the current AI-predicted sepsis risk score is hard to interpret. Participants do not understand why an obvious sepsis case has a lower risk score than the score of a less obvious case. They found that their multiple years of medical experience could not help interpret the relativeness of the score or the factors contributing to a score.

original.label: §3.2.4 No Actionable Insights
original.text: Our interviewees also questioned the limited utility of it in their medical diagnostic process of sepsis. Most participants (P1, P2, P3, P4, P6) mentioned that they simply ignored the risk score during their diagnosis process because they were confused about the purpose of the risk score and did not know what to do as an actionable next step given a high or low risk score.

original.label: §3.2.5 AI Helper or AI Challenger?
original.text: The current AI-based sepsis prediction module mainly focuses on the prediction of the final decision outcomes, in our case, it is the sepsis risk score that suggests whether a patient has sepsis. Such output at the final decision stage implicitly "challenges" human decision-makers' authority and expertise in their roles of making that final decision. Due to the current prediction focusing too much on the final stage of decision making only, this human-AI interaction paradigm is essentially perceived as a human-AI competition, which challenges the human experts' expertise and intimidates their authorities and feelings.

original.label: §4.1 Design Strategies
original.text: Based on the stage 1 findings, we conclude five design strategies for the new design of the sepsis module. Design Strategy 1: Performing Future Risk Score Prediction. Design Strategy 2: Providing Accessible Model Explanation. Design Strategy 3: Revealing Actionable Insights and Suggestions. Design Strategy 4: Displaying Uncertainty beyond the Risk Score. Design Strategy 5: Shifting AI from Suggesting the Final Decision to Supporting Intermediate Stages.

original.label: §4.2 Future Risk Prediction with Uncertainty Visualization
original.text: As mentioned in Section 4.1, one core part of SepsisLab is a predictive algorithm that generates future prediction of sepsis risk scores. As shown in the bottom part of SepsisLab interface, we design a time-series plot to visualize both the historical (the solid line) and expected future (the dashed line) risk prediction trajectory over time.

original.label: §4.2 Uncertainty Visualization
original.text: Moreover, for each risk score prediction, the model also generates the uncertainty range of the expected value, as shown in the gray area in the line plot. We selected visualized confidence intervals to display the prediction uncertainty, because prior work has found that confidence intervals evoke high levels of trust. While prior research has found that other uncertainty visualization techniques produce more accurate risk judgments, our visualization aims to show relative risk rather than the precise values.

original.label: §4.2 Lab Tests Recommendations
original.text: Combining the two parts of future risk prediction and feature important visualization, we added the lab tests recommendation function into our interface. SepsisLab recommends an item list ranked by their importance. The clinician can select one or multiple lab items and observe how their test results could influence the model's risk prediction trajectory (the red dashed line) and the corresponding uncertainty range (the red area). Note that the red line and area are counterfactual values that are estimated by the algorithm.

original.label: §4.3.1 LSTM-based Predictive Model
original.text: Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) model architectures are more suitable for the sepsis predictive tasks on the temporal observational data with irregular time intervals. In this work, we select Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) as the backbone of the prediction framework, which is able to capture both long-term and short-term clinical information in patients' EHR history. A variable attention module that can handle varying numbers of inputs is followed to generate a fixed-size vector that is sent to LSTM. The attention module can automatically focus on important variables, and the learned attention weights can be used to interpret the prediction results — this enables the users to see each input feature's importance score contributing to the predictive model.

original.label: §4.3.2 Lab Test Recommendation Based on Uncertainty Estimation
original.text: Based on previous work that has shown superior performance in missing value imputation with deep learning, we adopt Monte Carlo Simulation (MCS) to sample the missing values many times and compute the uncertainty with the standard deviation of the outputs with MCS. Two thresholds th_s and th_e (1 > th_s, th_e > 0) are set according to the desired sensitivity and precision to decide whether new laboratory values should be requested. We define the uncertainty as Shannon entropy e = -p log(p) - (1-p) log(1-p). If the uncertainty e > th_e, the model will recommend clinicians to collect more clinical variables, for example laboratory values.

original.label: §4.3.2 Results (Table 2)
original.text: To confirm that the recommendation can improve the model performance in the absence of certain laboratory values, we conducted experimental tests on the MIMIC-III dataset. As shown in Table 2, in our implementation, with an LSTM model, our recommendation algorithm performs comparable to full observation setting models and outperforms the masked setting models by approximately 10% with only 9.6% extra laboratory values requested by our recommendation algorithm. The results indicate that our recommendation algorithm can achieve performance nearly equivalent to that under full observation, thus enabling accurate predictions even with fewer lab test results, without compromising prediction precision.

original.label: §5.2.1 Shifting AI Focus Enhances Collaboration
original.text: Our evaluation study results confirmed that the new system was able to improve the human-AI collaboration experience. Clinicians (P2, P6) commented that the early prediction with uncertainty visualization and lab test recommendations could ease feelings of being in competition with or replaced by AI, since it no longer focuses on the final stage and leaves humans to decide whether to take the suggestions or not. Instead, our system ensures that physicians retain the role of final decision-maker. "if you tell me, you (model) need these labs, you got it, buddy; I will order those labs. It's not a problem..." (P2)

original.label: §6.2 Beyond Sepsis Diagnosis
original.text: We envision such a new human-AI collaboration paradigm can move beyond sepsis diagnosis. In healthcare, there are a number of decision-making tasks that have similar properties as sepsis diagnosis: highly uncertain, high-stakes, and time-sensitive. Examples include both physical health problems (e.g., stroke, heart attack, and meningitis), and mental health problems (e.g., major depressive disorder with suicidal ideation, bipolar disorder during a manic episode, and schizophrenia with psychosis). In addition, we foresee that such a new human-AI collaboration paradigm can be applicable to other non-healthcare complex decision-making scenarios as well, such as military (e.g., hostage rescues, evacuation operations), business (e.g., product launch/recalls, market crisis), emergency response (e.g., earthquake response, wildfire response), just name a few.

original.label: §6.3 Risks and Ethical Concerns
original.text: Despite the promising advantage of our newly proposed human-AI collaboration paradigm, we also want to highlight the risks and ethical concerns associated with it. For example, such a new paradigm would introduce additional burden to experts. In our study, participants were concerned about the cognitive load caused by our system, which has been reported in previous studies related to visualization. Meanwhile, mistakes and errors made by AI are inevitable, and the potential biases embedded in AI algorithms are not yet addressed by this new paradigm. Besides, there is a potential risk of over-dependence on AI systems, which might foster complacency and reduce vigilance among human experts.

original.label: §6.4 Limitation and Future Work
original.text: There are several limitations in our work. First, our study population is limited. We only involved six physicians in our formative study and heuristic evaluation, who came from the same hospital and only used one specific sepsis module ESM. Second, our system is implemented as a prototype and not integrated into the EHR system. Our evaluation study used our system as a design probe to collect clinicians' feedback. We also find that physicians and nurses often have different responsibilities in the diagnostic decision-making process. Different workflows that arise from different clinical roles are often overlooked in contemporary AI-CDSS designs.

original.label: §2.2 Challenges of AI-empowered Clinical Decision Support
original.text: With AI's advancement, many algorithms have been crafted to bolster clinical decision making, aiming to improve patient outcomes and reduce clinician workload, and numerous studies show such AI-CDSS can effectively assist doctors. However, a significant hurdle is liability: because clinicians bear responsibility for medical decisions, they approach AI predictions with caution, and the opaque nature of AI makes it hard for doctors to fully embrace its direct diagnostic and treatment suggestions. Recent research even suggests that embedding AI into EHR systems might inadvertently increase physician workload, and a disconnection exists between what clinicians expect from AI and what AI actually delivers.

original.label: §2.3 AI-supported Clinical Decision Support Systems Design
original.text: There has been growing research on designing AI-CDSS systems. Typically, AI-CDSS offers decision support by delivering predictions, risk evaluations, or suggestions, and some systems offer supplementary evidence or explanations to help doctors understand AI output and calibrate trust — referencing literature, comparing with prior data, or bridging knowledge gaps. Prior research suggests that in sepsis diagnosis, the most important challenge is not to use XAI methods to explain the model outcome, but to use methods consistent with the doctor's cognition to establish trust between the doctor and the model. However, many current explainable-AI methods cannot meet this goal for sepsis-related AI-CDSS.

original.label: §3.1 Formative Study — Method
original.text: We recruited six clinicians who are domain experts whose daily work involves sepsis diagnosis and who have used the Epic Sepsis Module (ESM). We used snowball sampling to recruit them; all are active physicians or nurses in the Intensive Care Unit, Emergency Department, or Internal Medicine, where clinicians most likely encounter sepsis patients. All interview sessions were conducted remotely via Zoom and lasted, on average, 35 minutes, and the study was pre-approved by the IRB. During the interviews, participants were asked to recall a recent sepsis case and detail how the diagnosis decision was made.

original.label: §3.3 Summary of Results
original.text: In summary, our formative study shows that the existing AI-driven sepsis risk prediction module does not support clinicians in their decision-making, because the current algorithm is belated and inaccurate, the interface lacks explanations, and the prediction cannot be transformed into a diagnostic or treatment action. These challenges reveal a fundamental issue of the existing human-AI decision-making paradigm: human experts need AI to focus more on supporting their intermediate decision-making process, rather than predicting a final outcome.

original.label: §4.3.3 Counterfactual Prediction
original.text: From AI's perspective, it would love clinicians to perform all kinds of lab tests so it can reduce most of its uncertainty and predict more accurately; but from the human's perspective, that is too costly and inhumane. While the lab-test recommendation can identify the most informative missing test, it is still hard for doctors to intuitively see the value of these lab tests. Therefore, SepsisLab computes a counterfactual prediction: it estimates how the risk trajectory and its uncertainty would change if a recommended lab test were performed — letting clinicians weigh the benefit before paying the time and cost of ordering it.

original.label: §5.1 Evaluation — Design and Procedure
original.text: We recruited the same six clinical experts to perform a heuristic test with our system. We first pre-loaded de-identified patient data from MIMIC-III into our prototype and deployed it on an internal cloud cluster to prevent leakage of patient data. To demonstrate the system across varied conditions, we selected two patient groups — one ultimately diagnosed with sepsis and one without — and randomly chose five data points from each, totaling ten cases for participants to view and interact with. Our SepsisLab system was used as a design probe to solicit participants' user experience and feedback.

original.label: §5.2.2 Future Prediction & Visualization Reveals More
original.text: All six participants liked the time-based prediction capability of our system. The prediction graph, with its uncertainty visualization, went beyond the final decision stage and provided much richer information than a single risk score — "I love that graph because it says the whole picture" (P1). Participants felt this could help them understand the model better, inspect whether it is reliable, and see not only where the patient is now but where the risk is heading hours from now.

original.label: §5.2.3 Lab Test Recommendation Provides Actionable Insights
original.text: All six participants responded positively to the lab test recommendation. They confirmed that the recommendations were consistent with current physician workflows, which validates the design, and said they would take advantage of the function to consider and act on lab tests. As P6 put it, knowing which lab results would help the AI make a better prediction would make them give it a try — and those results might also be more useful in their own decision.

original.label: §5.2.4 Counterfactual Information Improves Transparency
original.text: Participants agreed that showing counterfactual predictions based on the recommendation would drive their choice of lab tests. Traditionally they relied on personal experience, yet sepsis is hard to diagnose due to its inherent uncertainty; being able to see the potential outcomes of different lab tests would help their thinking process and narrow the search space — for example, prompting them to repeat a lactate or a white-blood-count when the patient is scoring high.

original.label: §6.1 Human-AI Collaboration in High-Stakes, Time-Sensitive Decisions
original.text: With sepsis as an example, our work reveals a fundamental problem in the existing human-AI collaboration paradigm for high-uncertainty, high-stakes, and time-sensitive decisions. Most AI research in this space aims to create the most accurate risk-score prediction model, but this goal fails to form effective human-AI collaboration: physicians found such a model unhelpful and intimidating, challenging their role as the final decision-maker. Instead, we argue that AI should support human experts in the intermediate stages (Steps 1–3) rather than the final stage, positioning AI to support the decision process without displacing the expert's role.

original.label: §7 Conclusion
original.text: In this work, we aim to design a better human-AI collaboration paradigm to support human experts in high-uncertainty, high-stakes, and time-sensitive decision-making tasks, focusing on sepsis diagnosis. Through a formative study with six physicians, we found the existing module not only useless but a source of extra meaningless workload, revealing a fundamental problem: AI should not focus on predicting the final decision, which can intimidate human experts. Based on these insights we built SepsisLab, which shifts the AI's focus from the final stage to the intermediate stages, and our evaluation suggests it enables a more promising human-AI collaboration for sepsis and other high-stakes medical decisions.

annotations.content: the forecasting engine

annotations.anchor: future prediction of sepsis risk scores
annotations.content: The old tool answered 'is it sepsis now?'. This answers 'where is this going in the next few hours?'
A line chart of risk across hours — easy to read at a glance.
what has happened so far
Solid = history, dashed = prediction. The dashed part is the whole point: it buys time to act early.
annotations.example: E.g. solid stops at now (risk 13); dashed climbs to ~30 over the next 10 hours → act early.

annotations.content: Not 'risk is 30%' but 'around 30%, give or take' — honesty about how sure the model is.
The wider the band, the less sure the model — shown around the forecast line.
A common way to draw uncertainty; chosen here because studies show people trust it.
They picked this picture because it makes doctors trust the tool — adoption matters as much as accuracy.
annotations.gloss: a design choice for trust, not math

annotations.content: A ranking of which lab values are driving the prediction — doubles as the model's explanation.

annotations.content: Ordered by how much each test would shrink the model's uncertainty.

annotations.gloss: a 'what if I ran this test?' preview
annotations.example: E.g. you havent run a lactate test → it previews: ‘this would likely push risk from 13 to 25’.

annotations.content: Built to process things in order, feeding each step's memory into the next — good for a timeline.
Real records are messy — readings come at odd hours, some not at all. The model must cope with gaps.
A neural net that holds onto what mattered earlier — so last night's reading still informs today's.
Handles a varying number of inputs and learns what to focus on — also produces the explanation.
The 'focus' numbers double as a plain-language reason for the prediction.

annotations.content: labs not taken yet

50/50 coin-flip → entropy high; 99% sure either way → near 0.
annotations.example: E.g. 50/50 coin-flip → entropy ≈ 1 (max doubt); 99% sure → entropy ≈ 0.

annotations.content: When too unsure, the system names the one test that would reduce the doubt most.

annotations.anchor: not integrated into the EHR system
annotations.content: It was tested as a mock-up, so real-world behaviour may differ.
annotations.gloss: a prototype, not a live tool

annotations.content: The prototype's job was to provoke reactions and surface needs, not to prove performance.
A parting insight: one-size-fits-all interfaces overlook distinct clinical roles.
annotations.gloss: nurses vs doctors need different tools

annotations.content: Because the doctor is legally on the hook for the outcome, they can't just defer to a model — so trust must be earned.
You can't see why the model said what it said — which makes a cautious clinician reluctant to follow it.
Explainable-AI: techniques meant to show why a model predicted something. The point here: technical explanations aren't enough.
An explanation only builds trust if it lines up with how the clinician already reasons — not just a feature-importance chart.
Recruit a few fitting people, then ask them to refer others like them — common for hard-to-reach experts.
Institutional Review Board — the ethics committee that must approve studies involving people.
A 'what if' estimate: the model re-forecasts as though a test had been run, so you see the payoff before ordering it.
You can't run every lab on a patient — so the system has to justify which one is worth it.
A large, de-identified public database of real ICU records — widely used to test clinical models.
Use the prototype as a prompt to draw out reactions and needs, rather than as a finished product under formal test.
Showing risk over time as a curve, not one number — the feature clinicians responded to most.
Steps 1–3 — hunch, gather, test — versus step 4, the final call. The paper's central claim about where AI belongs.
AI-supported Clinical Decision Support System — software that helps doctors decide, the broad category this work sits in.
The steps before the verdict — where the formative study says help is actually wanted.
It fit how doctors already order labs — so it felt usable, not an extra chore.
See which test would move the prediction, so you rule options in or out faster.
The recurring thesis: put AI in the steps before the decision, not on the decision itself.
